author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XVIII. 7.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Woe unto the world because of offenses:for it must needs be that offenses come: but woe to that man bywhom the offense cometh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And if ‘it must needs be that offenses come,’” (some one of our adversaries may perchance say), “why doth He lament over the world, when He ought rather to afford succor, and to stretch forth His hand in its behalf?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this were the part of a physician, and a protector, whereas the other might be looked for even from any ordinary person.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then could we possibly say, in answer to so shameless a tongue? nay what dost thou seek for equal to this healing care of His?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed being God He became man for thee, and took the form of a slave, and underwent all extremities, and left undone none of those things which it concerned Him to do.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But inasmuch as unthankful men were nothing the better for this, He laments over them, for that after so much fostering care they continued in their unsoundness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was like as if over the sick man, that had had the advantage of much attendance, and who had not been willing to obey the rules of the physician, any one were to lament and say, “Woe to such a man from his infirmity, which he has increased by his own remissness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in that case indeed there is no advantage from the bewailing, but here this too is a kind of healing treatment to foretell what would be, and to lament it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many oftentimes, though, when advised, they were nothing profited, yet, when mourned for, they amended.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For which reason most of all He used the word “Woe,” thoroughly to rouse them, and to make them in earnest, and to work upon them to be wakeful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And at the same time He shows forth the good will He had towards those very men and His own mildness, that He mourns for them even when gainsaying, not taking mere disgust at it, but correcting them, both with the mourning, and with the prediction, so as to win them over.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But how is this possible? he may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if “it must needs be that offenses come,” how is it possible to escape these?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because that the offenses come indeed must needs be, but that men should perish is not altogether of necessity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Like as though a physician should say (for nothing hinders our using the same illustration again), it must needs be that this disease should come on, but it is not a necessary consequence that he who gives heed should be of course destroyed by the disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He said, as I mentioned, to awaken together with the others His disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that they may not slumber, as sent unto peace and unto untroubled life, He shows many wars close upon them, from without, from within.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Declaring this, Paul said, “Without were fightings, within were fears;”and, “In perils among false brethren;”and in his discourse to the Milesians too He said, “Also of you shall some arise speaking perverse things;”and He Himself too said, “The man’s foes shall be they of his own household.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when He said, “It must needs be,” it is not as taking away the power of choosing for themselves, nor the freedom of the moral principle, nor as placing man’s life under any absolute constraint of circumstances, that He saith these things, but He foretells what would surely be; and this Luke hath set forth in another form of expression, “It is impossible but that offenses should come.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what are the offenses?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The hindrances on the right way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus also do those on the stage call them that are skilled in those matters, them that distort their bodies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is not then His prediction that brings the offenses; far from it; neither because He foretold it, therefore doth it take place; but because it surely was to be, therefore He foretold it; since if those who bring in the offenses had not been minded to do wickedly, neither would the offenses have come; and if they had not been to come, neither would they have been foretold.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But because those men did evil, and were incurably diseased, the offenses came, and He foretells that which is to be.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if these men had been kept right, it may be said, and there had been no one to bring in an offense, would not this saying have been convicted of falsehood?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means, for neither would it have been spoken.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if all were to have been kept right, He would not have said, “it must needs be that they come,” but because He foreknew they would be of themselves incorrigible, therefore He said, the offenses will surely come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore did He not take them out of the way? it may be said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, wherefore should they have been taken out of the way?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the sake of them that are hurt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not thence is the ruin of them that are hurt, but from their own remissness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the virtuous prove it, who, so far from being injured thereby, are even in the greatest degree profited, such as was Job, such as was Joseph, such as were all the righteous, and the apostles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if many perish, it is from their own slumbering.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if it were not so, but the ruin was the effect of the offenses, all must have perished.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if there are those who escape, let him who doth not escape impute it to himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the offenses, as I have said, awaken, and render more quick-sighted, and sharper, not only him that is preserved; but even him that hath fallen into them, if he rise up again quickly, for they render him more safe, and make him more difficult to overcome; so that if we be watchful, no small profit do we reap from hence, even to be continually awake.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if when we have enemies, and when so many dangers are pressing upon us, we sleep, what should we be if living in security.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, if thou wilt, look at the first man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if having lived in paradise a short time, perchance not so much as a whole day, and having enjoyed delights, he drove on to such a pitch of wickedness, as even to imagine an equality with God, and to account the deceiver a benefactor, and not to keep to one commandment; if he had lived the rest of his life also without affliction, what would he not have done?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when we say these things, they make other objections again, asking, And why did God make him such?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God did not make him such, far from it, since then neither would He have punished him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we in those matters in which we are the cause, do not find fault with our servant, much more will not the God of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But whence did this come to pass?” one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of himself and his own remissness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What means, of himself?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Ask thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it be not of themselves the bad are bad, do not punish thy servant nor reprove thy wife for what errors she may commit, neither beat thy son, nor blame thy friend, nor hate thine enemy that doth despite to thee: for all these deserve to be pitied, not to be punished, unless they offend of themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But I am not able to practise self-restraint,” one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet, when thou perceivest the cause not to be with them, but of another necessity, thou canst practise self-restraint.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When at least a servant being taken with sickness doth not the things enjoined him, so far from blaming thou dost rather excuse him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus thou art a witness, that the one thing is of one’s self, the other not of one’s self.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that here too, if thou knewest that he was wicked from being born such, so far from blaming, thou wouldest rather have shown him indulgence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For surely, when thou makest him allowance for his illness, it could not be that thou wouldest have refused to make allowance for God’s act of creation, if indeed he had been made such from the very first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in another way too it is easy to stop the mouths of such men, for great is the abounding power of the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wherefore dost thou never find fault with thy servant, because he is not of a beautiful countenance, that he is not of fine stature in his body, that he is not able to fly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because these things are natural.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So then from blame against his nature he is acquitted, and no man gainsays it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore thou blamest, thou showest that the fault is not of nature but of his choice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if in those things, which we do not blame, we bear witness that the whole is of nature, it is evident that where we reprove, we declare that the offense is of the choice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not then bring forward, I beseech thee, perverse reasonings, neither sophistries and webs slighter than the spider’s, but answer me this again: Did God make all men?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is surely plain to every man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then are not all equal in respect of virtue and vice? whence are the good, and gentle, and meek? whence are the worthless and evil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if these things do not require any purpose, but are of nature, how are the one this, the others that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if by nature all were bad, it were not possible for any one to be good, but if good by nature, then no one bad.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if there were one nature of all men, they must needs in this respect be all one, whether they were to be this, or whether they were to be that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if we should say that by nature the one are good, the other bad, which would not be reasonable (as we have shown), these things must be unchangeable, for the things of nature are unchangeable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All mortals are also liable to suffering; and no one is free from suffering, though he strive without end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now we see of good many becoming worthless, and of worthless good, the one through remissness, the other by earnestness; which thing most of all indicates that these things do not come of nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the things of nature are neither changed, nor do they need diligence for their acquisition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For like as for seeing and hearing we do not need labor, so neither should we need toils in virtue, if it had been apportioned by nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But wherefore did He at all make worthless men, when He might have made all men good?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence then are the evil things?” saith he.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Ask thyself; for it is my part to show they are not of nature, nor from God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Come they then of themselves?” he saith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But are they unoriginated?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Speak reverently, O man, and start back from this madness, honoring with one honor God and the evil things, and that honor the highest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they be unoriginate they are mighty, and cannot so much as be plucked up, nor pass into annihilation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that what is unoriginate is imperishable, is surely manifest to all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whence also are there so many good, when evil hath such great power? how are they that have an origin stronger than that which is unoriginate?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But God destroys these things,” he saith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how will He destroy what are of equal honor, and of equal strength, and of the same age, as one might say, with Himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Oh malice of the devil! how great an evil hath he invented!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With what blasphemy hath he persuaded men to surround God! with what cloak of godliness hath he devised another profane account?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For desiring to show, that not of Him was the evil, they brought in another evil doctrine, saying, that these things are unoriginate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Whence then are evils?” one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: From willing and not willing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But the very thing of our willing and not willing, whence is it?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: From ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou dost the same in asking, as if when thou hadst asked, whence is seeing and not seeing? then when I said, from closing the eyes or not closing the eyes, thou wert to ask again; the very closing the eyes or not, whence is it? then having heard that it was of ourselves, and our will, thou wert to seek again another cause.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For evil is nothing else than disobedience to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Whence then,” one may say, “did man find this?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why, was it a task to find this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I pray thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Nay, neither do I say this, that this thing is difficult; but whence became he desirous to disobey.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “From remissness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For having power for either, he inclined rather to this.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou art perplexed yet and dizzy at hearing this, I will ask thee nothing difficult nor involved, but a simple and plain question.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou become some time bad? and hast thou become some time also good?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What I mean, is like this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou prevail some time over passion, and wast thou taken again by passion?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou been overtaken by drunkenness, and hast thou prevailed over drunkenness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wast thou once moved to wrath, and again not moved to wrath?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou overlook a poor man, and not overlook him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou commit whoredom once? and didst thou become chaste again?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence then are all these things? tell me, whence?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay if thou thyself do not tell, I will say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because at one time thou didst restrain thyself and strive, but after that thou becamest remiss and careless.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to those that are desperate, and are continually in wickedness, and are in a state of senselessness, and are mad, and who are not willing so much as to hear what will amend them, I will not even discourse of self restraint; but to them that have been sometimes in the one, and sometimes in the other, I will gladly speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou once take by violence the things that belonged not to thee; and after this, subdued by pity, didst impart even of thine unto him that was in need?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence then this change?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not quite plain it is from the mind, and the choice of will?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is quite plain, and there is no one who would not say this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I entreat you to be in earnest, and to cleave to virtue, and ye will have no need of these questions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For our evils are mere names, if we be willing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Inquire not then whence are evils, neither perplex thyself; but having found that they are from remissness only, flee the evil deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if any one should say, that these things come not from us; whenever thou seest him angry with his servants, and provoked with his wife, and blaming a child, and condemning them who injure him, say to him, how then saidst thou, that evils come not from us?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they be not from us, wherefore dost thou find fault?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Say again; is it of thyself thou revilest, and insultest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if it be not of thyself, let no man be angry with thee; but if it be of thyself, of thyself and of thy remissness are thy evil deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what? thinkest thou there are some good men?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if indeed no man is good, whence hast thou this word? whence are praises?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if there are good men, it is quite plain that they will also reprove the bad.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet if no one is voluntarily wicked, nor of himself, the good will be found to be unjustly reproving the bad, and they themselves too will be in this way bad again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what can be worse than to subject the guiltless to accusations?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if they continue in our estimation good men, though reproving, and this especially is a proof of their goodness, even to the very fools it is hereby plain, that no one is ever by necessity bad.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if after all this thou wouldest still inquire, whence are evils?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I would say, from remissness, from idleness, from keeping company with the bad, from contempt of virtue; hence are both the evils themselves, and the fact that some inquire, whence are the evils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since of them surely who do right no one inquires about these things, of them that are purposed to live equitably and temperately; but they, who dare to commit wicked acts, and wish to devise some foolish comfortto themselves by these discussions, do weave spiders’ webs.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But let us tear these in pieces not by our words only, but by our deeds too.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither are these things of necessity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they were of necessity, He would not have said, “Woe to the man, by whom the offense cometh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For those only doth he bewail, who are wicked by their choice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if He saith “by whom,”marvel not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not as though another were bringing in it by him, doth He say this, but viewing him as himself causing the whole.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the Scripture is wont to say, “by whom,” for “of whom;”as when it saith, “I have gotten a man by God,”putting not the second cause, but the first; and again, “Is not the interpretation of them by God,”and, “God is faithful, by whom ye are called unto the fellowship of His Son.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that thou mayest learn that it is not of necessity, hear also what follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after bewailing them, He saith, “If thy hand, or thy foot offend thee, cut them off, and cast them from thee: for it is better for thee to enter into life halt or maimed, rather than having two hands or feet to be cast into the fire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thy right eye offend thee, pluck it out; it is better for thee to enter into life with one eye, than having two eyes to be cast into the furnace of fire;”not saying these things of limbs; far from it; but of friends, of relations, whom we regard in the rank of necessary members.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This He had both said further back, and now He saith it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing is so hurtful as bad company.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what things compulsion cannot, friendship can often effect, both for hurt, and for profit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore with much earnestness He commands us to cut off them that hurt us, intimating these that bring the offenses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how He hath put away the mischief that would result from the offenses?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By foretelling that there surely will be offenses, so that they might find no one in a state of carelessness, but that looking for them men might be watchful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By showing the evils to be great (for He would not have said without purpose, “Woe to the world because of the offenses,” but to show that great is the mischief therefrom), by lamenting again in stronger terms over him that brings them in.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the saying, “But woe to that man,” was that of one showing that great was the punishment, but not this only, but also by the comparison which He added He increased the fear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then He is not satisfied with these things, but He showeth also the way, by which one may avoid the offenses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The wicked, saith He, though they be exceeding dear friends to thee, cut off from thy friendship.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He giveth a reason that cannot be gainsaid.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they continue friends, thou wilt not gain them, but thou wilt lose thyself besides; but if thou shouldest cut them off, thine own salvation at least thou wilt gain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that if any one’s friendship harms thee, cut it off from thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if of our own members we often cut off many, when they are both in an incurable state, and are ruining the rest, much more ought one to do this in the case of friends.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if evils were by nature, superfluous were all this admonition and advice, superfluous the precaution by the means that have been mentioned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if it be not superfluous, as surely it is not superfluous, it is quite clear that wickedness is of the will.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Take heed that ye despise not one of these little ones; for I say unto you, that their angels do always behold the face of my Father which is in Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He calleth little ones not them that are really little, but them that are so esteemed by the multitude, the poor, the objects of contempt, the unknown (for how should he be little who is equal in value to the whole world; how should he be little, who is dear to God?); but them who in the imagination of the multitude are so esteemed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He speaks not of many only, but even of one, even by this again warding off the hurt of the many offenses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even as to flee the wicked, so also to honor the good, hath very great gain, and would be a twofold security to him who gives heed, the one by rooting out the friendships with them that offend, the other from regarding these saints with respect and honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then in another way also He makes them objects of reverence, saying, “That their angels do always behold the face of my Father which is in Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence it is evident, that the saints have angels, or even all men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the apostle too saith of the woman, “That she ought to have power on her head because of the angels.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Moses, “He set the bounds of the nations according to the number of the angelsof God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But here He is discoursing not of angels only, but rather of angels that are greater than others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when He saith, “The face of my Father,” He means nothing else than their fuller confidence, and their great honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For the Son of Man is come to save that which was lost.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, He is putting another reason stronger than the former, and connects with it a parable, by which He brings in the Father also as desiring these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For how think ye?” saith He; “If a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray, doth he not leave the ninety and nine, and goeth into the mountains, and seeketh that which is gone astray?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if so be that he find it,he rejoiceth over it more than over the ninety and nine, which went not astray.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Even so it is not will before your Father,that one of these little ones should perish.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou by how many things He is urging to the care of our mean brethren.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Say not then, “Such a one is a blacksmith, a shoemaker, he is a ploughman, he is a fool,” and so despise him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in order that thou shouldest not feel this, see by how many motives He persuades thee to practise moderation, and presses thee into a care for these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He set a little child, and saith, “Be ye as little children.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And, “Whosoever receiveth such a little child receiveth me;” and, “Whosoever shall offend,” shall suffer the utmost penalties.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He was not even satisfied with the comparison of the “millstone,” but added also His “woe,” and commanded us to cut off such, though they be in the place of hands and eyes to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And by the angels again that are entrusted with these same mean brethren, He makes them objects of veneration, and from His own will and passion (for when He said, “The Son of Man is come to save that which was lost,” He signifies even the cross, like as Paul saith, speaking of a brother, “For whom Christ died”); and from the Father, for that neither to Him doth it seem good that one should perish; and from common custom, because the shepherd leaves them that are safe, and seeks what is lost; and when he hath found what was gone astray, he is greatly delighted at the finding and the saving of this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If then God thus rejoices over the little one that is found, how dost thou despise them that are the objects of God’s earnest care, when one ought to give up even one’s very life for one of these little ones?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But is he weak and mean?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore for this very cause most of all, one ought to do everything in order to preserve him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even He Himself left the ninety and nine sheep, and went after this, and the safety of so many availed not to throw into the shade the loss of one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Luke saith, that He even brought it on his shoulders, and that “There was greater joy over one sinner that repenteth, than over ninety and nine just persons.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And from His forsaking those that were saved for it, and from His taking more pleasure in this one, He showed His earnestness about it to be great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then be careless about such souls as these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For all these things are said for this object.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by threatening, that he who has not become a little child should not so much as at all set foot in the Heavens, and speaking of “the millstone,” He hath brought down the haughtiness of the boastful; for nothing is so hostile to love as pride; and by saying, “It must needs be that offenses come,” He made them to be wakeful; and by adding, “Woe unto him by whom the offense cometh,” He hath caused each to endeavor that it be not by him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while by commanding to cut off them that offend He made salvation easy; by enjoining not to despise them, and not merely enjoining, but with earnestness (for “take heed,” saith He, “that ye despise not one of these little ones”), and by saying, “Their angels behold the face of my Father,” and, “For this end am I come,” and “my Father willeth this,” He hath made those who should take care of them more diligent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou what a wall He hath set around them, and what earnest care He taketh of them that are contemptible and perishing, at once threatening incurable ills to them that make them fall, and promising great blessings to them that wait upon them, and take care of them, and bringing an example from Himself again and from the Father?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Him let us also imitate, refusing none of the tasks that seem lowly and troublesome for our brethren’s sake; but though we have to do service, though he be small, though he be mean for whom this is done, though the work be laborious, though we must pass over mountains and precipices, let all things be held endurable for the salvation of our brother.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For a soul is an object of such earnest care to God, that “He spared not His own Son.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I entreat, when morning hath appeared, straightway as we come out of our house, let us have this one object in view, this earnest care above all, to rescue him that is in danger; I do not mean this danger only that is known by sense, for this is not danger at all, but the danger of the soul, that which is brought upon men by the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the merchant too, to increase his wealth, crosses the sea; and the artisan, to add to his substance, doeth all things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us also then not be satisfied with our own salvation only, since else we destroy even this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in a war too, and in an engagement, the soldier who is looking to this only how he may save himself by flight, destroys the rest also with himself; much as on the other hand the noble-minded one, and he who stands in arms in defense of the others, with the others preserves himself also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since then our state too is a war, and of all wars the bitterest, and an engagement and a battle, even as our King commanded us, so let us set ourselves in array in the engagement, prepared for slaughter, and blood, and murders, looking to salvation in behalf of all, and cheering them that stand, and raising up them that are down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed many of our brethren lie fallen in this conflict, having wounds, wallowing in blood, and there is none to heal, not any one of the people, not a priest, no one else, no one to stand by, no friend, no brother, but we look every man to his own things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By reason of this we maim our own interests also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the greatest confidence and means of approval is the not looking to our own things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore I say, are we weak and easy to be overcome both by men, and by the devil, because we seek the opposite to this, and lock not our shields one with another, neither are fortified with godly love, but seek for ourselves other motives of friendship, some from relationship, some from long acquaintance, some from community of interest, some from neighborhood; and from every cause rather are we friends, than from godliness, when one’s friendships ought to be formed upon this only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now the contrary is done; with Jews and with Greekswe sometimes become friends, rather than with the children of the church.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, saith he, because the one is worthless, but the other kind and gentle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dost thou call thy brother worthless, who art commanded not to call him so much as Raca?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And art thou not ashamed, neither dost thou blush, at exposing thy brother, thy fellow member, him that hath shared in the same birth with thee, that hath partaken of the same table?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou hast any brother after the flesh, if he should perpetrate ten thousand evil deeds, thou laborest to conceal him, and accountest thyself also to partake of the shame, when he is disgraced; but as to thy spiritual brother, when thou oughtest to free him from calumny, thou dost rather encompass him with ten thousand charges against him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why he is worthless and insufferable,” thou mayest say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay then for this reason become his friend, that thou mayest put an end to his being such a one, that thou mayest convert him, that thou mayest lead him back to virtue.—“But he obeys not,” thou wilt say, “neither doth he bear advice.”—Whence knowest thou it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What, hast thou admonished him, and attempted to amend him?—“I have admonished him often,” thou wilt say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How many times?—Oftentimes, both once, and a second time.—Oh!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is this often?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, if thou hadst done this throughout all the time, oughtest thou to grow weary, and to give it up?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not how God is always admonishing us, by the prophets, by the apostles, by the evangelists?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then? have we performed all? and have we been obedient in all things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Did He then cease admonishing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Did He hold His peace?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Doth He not say each day, “Ye cannot serve God, and mammon”and with many, the superfluity and the tyranny of wealth yet increases?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Doth He not cry aloud each day, “Forgive, and ye shall have forgiveness,”and we become wild beasts more and more?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Doth He not continually admonish to restrain desire, and to keep the mastery over wicked lust, and many wallow worse than swine in this sin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But nevertheless, He ceases not speaking.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then do we not consider these things with ourselves, and say that even with us God reasons, and abstains not from doing this, although we disobey Him in many things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore He said that, “Few are the saved.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if virtue in ourselves suffices not for our salvation, but we must take with us others too when we depart; when we have saved neither ourselves, nor others, what shall we suffer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence shall we have any more a hope of salvation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why do I blame for these things, when not even of them that dwell with us do we take any account, of wife, and children, and servants, but we have care of one thing instead of another, like drunken men, that our servants may be more in number, and may serve us with much diligence, and that our children may receive from us a large inheritance, and that our wife may have ornaments of gold, and costly garments, and wealth; and we care not at all for themselves, but for the things that belong to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither do we care for our own wife, nor provide for her, but for the things that belong to the wife; neither for the child, but for the things of the child.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And we do the same as if any one seeing a house in a bad state, and the walls giving way, were to neglect to raise up these, and to make up great fences round it without; or when a body was diseased, were not to take care of this, but were to weave for it gilded garments; or when the mistress was ill, were to give heed to the maidservants, and the looms, and the vessels in the house, and mind other things, leaving her to lie and moan.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is done even now, and when our soul is in evil and wretched case, and angry, and reviling, and lusting wrongly, and full of vainglory, and at strife, and dragged down to the earth, and torn by so many wild beasts, we neglect to drive away the passions from her, and are careful about house and servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while if a bear has escaped by stealth, we shut up our houses, and run along by the narrow passages, so as not to fall in with the wild beast; now while not one wild beast, but many such thoughts are tearing in pieces the soul, we have not so much as a feeling of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in the city we take so much care, as to shut up the wild beasts in solitary places and in cages, and neither at the senate house of the city, nor at the courts of justice, nor at the king’s palace, but far off somewhere at a distance do we keep them chained; but in the case of the soul, where the senate house is, where the King’s palace, where the court of justice is, the wild beasts are let loose, crying and making a tumult about the mind itself and the royal throne.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore all things are turned upside down, and all is full of disturbance, the things within, the things without, and we are in nothing different from a city thrown into confusion from being overrun by barbarians; and what takes place in us is as though a serpent were setting on a brood of sparrows, and the sparrows, with their feeble cries, were flying about every way affrighted, and full of trouble, without having any place whither to go and end their consternation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I entreat, let us kill the serpent, let us shut up the wild beasts, let us stifle them, let us slay them, and these wicked thoughts let us give over to the sword of the Spirit, lest the prophet threaten us also with such things as he threatened Judea, that “The wild asses shall dance there, and porcupines, and serpents.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there are, there are even men worse than wild asses, living as it were in the wilderness, and kicking; yea the more part of the youth amongst us is like this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed having wild lusts they thus leap, they kick, going about unbridled, and spend their diligence on no becoming object.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the fathers are to blame, who while they constrain the horsebreakers to discipline their horses with much attention, and suffer not the youth of the colt to go on long untamed, but put upon it both a rein, and all the rest, from the beginning; but their own young ones they overlook, going about for a long season unbridled, and without temperance; disgracing themselves, by fornications, and gamings, and continuings in the wicked theatres, when they ought before fornication to give him to a wife, to a wife chaste, and highly endued with wisdom; for she will both bring off her husband from his most disorderly course of life, and will be instead of a rein to the colt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed fornications and adulteries come not from any other cause, than from young men’s being unrestrained.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he have a prudent wife, he will take care of house and honor and character.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But he is young,” you say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I know it too.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if Isaac was forty years old when he took his bride, passing all that time of his life in virginity, much more ought young men under grace to practise this self-restraint.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But oh what grief!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Ye do not endure to take care of their chastity, but ye overlook their disgracing, defiling themselves, becoming accursed; as though ye knew not that the profit of marriage is to preserve the body pure, and if this be not so, there is no advantage of marriage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But ye do the contrary; when they are filled with countless stains, then ye bring them to marriage without purpose and without fruit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why I must wait,” thou wilt say, “that he may become approved, that he may distinguish himself in the affairs of the state;” but of the soul ye have no consideration, but ye overlook it as a cast-away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this reason all things are full of confusion, and disorder, and trouble, because this is made a secondary matter, because necessary things are neglected, but the unimportant obtain much forethought.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowest thou not, that thou canst do no such kindness to the youth, as to keep him pure from whorish uncleannness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing is equal to the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because, “What is a man profited,” saith He, “if he shall gain the whole world, but lose his own soul.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But because the love of money hath overturned and cast down all, and hath thrust aside the strict fear of God, having seized upon the souls of men, like some rebel chief upon a citadel; therefore we are careless both of our children’s salvation, and of our own, looking to one object only, that having become wealthier, we may leave riches to others, and these again to others after them, and they that follow these to their posterity, becoming rather a kind of passers on of our possessions and of our money, but not masters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence great is our folly; hence the free are less esteemed than the slaves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For slaves we reprove, if not for their sake, yet for our own; but the free enjoy not the benefit even of this care, but are more vile in our estimation than these slaves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why do I say, than our slaves?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For our children are less esteemed than cattle; and we take care of horses and asses rather than of children.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And should one have a mule, great is his anxiety to find the best groom, and not one either harsh, or dishonest, or drunken, or ignorant of his art; but if we have set a tutorover a child’s soul, we take at once, and at random, whoever comes in our way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet than this art there is not another greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what is equal to training the soul, and forming the mind of one that is young?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that hath this art, ought to be more exactly observant than any painter and any sculptor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we take no account of this, but look to one thing only, that he may be trained as to his tongue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to this again we have directed our endeavors for money’s sake.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not that he may be able to speak, but that he may get money, does he learn speaking; since if it were possible to grow rich even without this, we should have no care even for this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how great is the tyranny of riches? how it has seized upon all things, and having bound them like some slaves or cattle, drags them where it will?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what are we advantaged by such accusations against it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For we indeed shoot at it in words, but it prevails over us in deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, not even so shall we cease to shoot at it with words from our tongue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if any advance is made, both we are gainers and you; but if you continue in the same things, all our part at least hath been performed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But may God both deliver you from this disease, and cause us to glory in you, for to Him be glory, and dominion, world without end.
